timelines.notes: Fetched 2026-05-11. Aggregated across Al Jazeera's Bangladesh quota protests coverage (per user instruction). Cluster spec flags this subtopic as politically_sensitive: true, so all entries are deliberately neutrally framed and event-anchored. Scoping note: this subtopic overlaps with c2_b_bangladesh_transition (which covers the broader political transition arc). To differentiate, this c8_a timeline focuses on the protest movement itself — going more granular on protest mechanics (Bangla Blockade, razakar comment, the iconic Rangpur killing, the one-point demand pivot) — while skipping the post-fall transition arc (Khaleda Zia's later political activity, Yunus's election debate evolution, India extradition diplomacy, Hasina's November 2025 conviction) which sits in c2_b. Primary source for July-August 2024 day-by-day detail is AJ's Gen Z longform 'How Bangladesh's Gen Z protests brought down PM Sheikh Hasina' (https://www.aljazeera.com/news/longform/2024/8/7/how-bangladeshs-gen-z-protests-brought-down-pm-sheikh-hasina) which is structured as a chronological day-by-day account. Contributing articles: https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/7/15/dozens-wounded-as-students-protest-government-job-quotas-in-bangladesh ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/7/16/whats-behind-bangladeshs-violent-quota-protests ; https://www.aljazeera.com/economy/2024/7/18/who-are-the-protesters-demanding-an-end-to-job-quotas-in-bangladesh ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/liveblog/2024/7/18/bangladesh-quota-protests-live-students-vow-shutdown-as-11-more-killed ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/7/19/its-war-now-as-bangladesh-quota-protests-escalate-whats-next ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/7/21/bangladesh-court-scraps-most-job-quotas-that-caused-deadly-unrest-reports ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/liveblog/2024/7/21/bangladesh-quota-protests-live-top-court-scales-back-job-reservation ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/7/21/how-peaceful-bangladesh-quota-protests-morphed-into-nationwide-unrest ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/8/3/students-renew-bangladesh-protests-call-for-nationwide-civil-disobedience ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/8/5/timeline-sheikh-hasinas-reign-ends-after-15-years ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/8/5/world-reacts-to-bangladesh-pm-sheikh-hasinas-removal-from-power ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/8/5/sheikh-hasina-a-critical-misstep-and-the-end-of-15-years-of-rule ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/longform/2024/8/7/how-bangladeshs-gen-z-protests-brought-down-pm-sheikh-hasina ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/11/17/bangladeshs-yunus-seeks-time-says-election-roadmap-after-reforms ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/8/5/theyre-selling-the-revolution-bangladesh-a-year-after-hasinas-downfall . Opinion piece read for context only (no entry drawn): https://www.aljazeera.com/opinions/2024/7/23/bangladesh-protests-are-no-longer-about-the-quota-system . Articles dropped as out of cluster window per rule 5: https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2018/5/14/bangladesh-students-demand-abolition-of-job-quota-system (2018 — predates cluster). Date approximations per rule 4: Jul 7, 2024 (Bangla Blockade) — the Bangla Blockade movement began approximately July 7 with road blockades around Dhaka University expanding nationally over the following days; Jul 7 chosen as the most defensible specific anchor for the movement's start since AJ Gen Z longform describes early-July road blockades without naming an exact start day. Jul 1, 2024 (Dhaka U protests began) — Gen Z longform and AJ coverage anchor early-July as the start; Jul 1 used as defensible specific date per the longform's framing. Jul 14 (razakar comment) — well-documented as occurring at Hasina's July 14 press conference; verified across AJ and contemporaneous coverage. Aug 3 (one-point demand) — students' coordinating body announced the one-point demand at a Shaheed Minar rally on Aug 3, 2024. Two same-date entries on Jul 21 and on Aug 5 preserved as materially distinct events per rule. Source disagreements: Death toll evolved over time — AJ's Aug 4 entry cites 'nearly 100' as the day's toll consistent with AJ coverage that day; later UN figure (1,400) appears on CNN's Feb 12 2025 entry; both retained on respective dates per rule, not retroactively reconciled. Hasina's 'razakar' framing — Hasina later denied she had insulted protesters and said the term was misinterpreted; AJ entry reports her statement as it was made on July 14 without endorsing either characterization. The 'shoot on sight' framing for July 19 curfew orders comes from multiple AJ news pieces reporting government orders to security forces; the government did not formally use that phrase in its public communications. Public figures named: Hasina, Yunus — both public officials. Private individuals NOT named: the student killed at Begum Rokeya University in Rangpur on July 16 is widely identified in public reporting and has become a public symbol, but the entry refers to 'a student' to maintain consistency with rule 6 treatment of victims; the location and event are sufficient to anchor the moment. Other named victims of crackdown are similarly not individually named.

Fetched 2026-05-11. Aggregated across CNN's Bangladesh quota protests coverage (per user instruction). Same scoping note as t1: c8_a is differentiated from c2_b by focus on the protest movement itself rather than the post-fall transition arc. Contributing articles from user-provided list (in cluster window): https://www.cnn.com/2024/07/16/asia/bangladesh-protests-quote-intl-latam ; https://www.cnn.com/2024/07/19/asia/bangladesh-job-quota-protests-explainer-intl-hnk ; https://www.cnn.com/2024/07/21/asia/bangladesh-supreme-court-rolls-back-job-quotas-intl ; https://www.cnn.com/2024/08/04/asia/bangladesh-clashes-police-protesters-activists-intl ; https://www.cnn.com/2024/08/06/asia/bangladesh-protests-hasina-resignation-explainer-intl-hnk ; https://www.cnn.com/2024/08/12/asia/bangladesh-quota-protests-student-martyr-intl-hnk-dst ; https://www.cnn.com/2024/09/03/asia/bangladesh-house-mirrors-detention-center-intl-hnk-dst (Sep 3 Aynaghar revelation) ; https://www.cnn.com/2024/12/31/asia/bangladesh-march-hasina-student-protests-intl-hnk ; https://www.cnn.com/2025/02/05/asia/bangladesh-protest-hasina-house-intl-hnk ; https://www.cnn.com/2025/02/12/asia/un-rights-office-bangladesh-protests-crackdown-intl-latam . Additional CNN articles drawn from broader coverage to anchor protest milestones not in user-provided list: https://www.cnn.com/2024/08/05/asia/bangladesh-prime-minister-residence-stormed-intl ; https://www.cnn.com/2024/08/07/asia/muhammad-yunus-bangladesh-government-intl-hnk (covers Aug 8 swearing-in) ; https://www.cnn.com/2024/10/17/asia/bangladesh-arrest-warrant-sheikh-hasina-intl-hnk . Article dropped as out of cluster window per rule 5: https://www.cnn.com/2026/02/09/asia/bangladesh-election-candidates-old-guard-intl-hnk (Feb 2026 — postdates Dec 2025 cluster end). Date approximations per rule 4: Jul 17 (universities shut down nationwide) — CNN's Jul 16 piece and Jul 19 explainer frame the shutdown as occurring 'across that week'; Jul 17 used as the most defensible specific date for the formal closure orders. Aug 3 (one-point demand) — CNN coverage and broader contemporaneous reporting anchor the demand to Aug 3; entry retained. Aug 8 (Yunus sworn in) — Yunus arrived in Dhaka Aug 8 evening and was sworn in that night; well-established date. Sep 3 (Aynaghar revealed) — CNN piece is dated Sep 3, describing Yunus's earlier inspection visit; pegged to CNN article publication date as the moment the detention complex was publicly revealed. Two same-date entries on Aug 5 preserved as materially distinct (Hasina's flight, and the storming/looting of her residence — though concurrent these are distinct news developments). Source disagreements: Death toll figures — CNN's Aug 4 entry cites 'at least 91' for the single deadliest day, consistent with CNN day-of reporting (AJ reported 'nearly 100' the same day); CNN's Feb 12 2025 entry then cites the UN OHCHR figure of 'up to 1,400 killed' across the full crackdown period. Both contemporaneous and retrospective figures retained per rule. The Aynaghar disclosure (Sep 3) is reported per CNN's framing; the Bangladesh government's prior official position denied the existence of secret detention sites. Public figures named: Hasina, Yunus, Khaleda Zia, Mujibur Rahman (founding leader, deceased 1975, named in connection with his historic Dhaka home demolished by protesters Feb 5 2025) — all public officials/figures of historical record. The 'student martyr' referenced in the Aug 12 CNN piece is widely publicly identified but the entry refers to 'vandalised Hasina mural demanding accountability for killings' without naming individuals, per rule 6 consistency.
